text:
Innovation that reconciles local culture and traditions enables the conservation of unique cultural features of each place while ensuring global connectivity in a changing and evolving society, and this balance must be achieved in a way that guarantees the overall stability of the system. Based on this reasoning, this paper aims to reflect on the role of strategic design and balance, on the one hand, the needs of innovation and, on the other, those of local culture to respond effectively to the major cultural challenges of our time.

This research explores the potentialities of strategic design to foster Cosmopolitan Localism – a process of self-representation that recognizes local identities and diversity through the establishment of inclusive relationships among rural communities and external agents. This research has a two-stage structure. Initially, it refers to the formulation of a theoretical framework based on a critical review of literature concerning design strategy — including products, services, and branding — and cultural sustainability in the context of social innovation. Subsequently, this framework is developed through an analysis of two cases studies in Argentina: SHOKAY, social enterprise of sustainable fashion and Chinese Country Fair, community-supported agriculture (CSA) project. These initiatives show that strategic design solutions are an effective tool to generate cosmopolitan localism because they enable the simple and tangible management of universal and lasting social issues while taking into account the specificities of the territories involved.

Design is a strategy that has become a necessary principle for our age. This article tries to give us an understanding of how design could serve as a framework to address certain cultural issues as an evolutionary, dynamic process and open new perspectives in relation to sustainable development.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
